metadata.summary: Every headless agent CLI must set four guards: print flag, allowlist, max-turns, closed stdin. Missing any guard causes hangs or runaway loops in production.

content.title: Headless CLI — Four Guards Against Mystery Hangs
content.summary: Every non-interactive run of an agent CLI (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Aider, opencode) MUST set four guards: (1) print/headless flag to disable the TUI, (2) explicit allowedTools allowlist (NEVER --dangerously-skip-permissions in prod), (3) --max-turns cap, (4) closed stdin via < /dev/null so the agent cannot block waiting on user input. Missing any one of the four turns into a mystery hang or runaway-loop incident in production.

why.p: Production CIs, cron jobs, and queue workers do not have a TTY, do not have a human to approve a prompt, and do not have a sane upper bound on how many turns the agent will take. Each missing guard maps to a known failure mode: TUI mode leads to process hangs waiting for terminal control codes; missing allowlist causes hangs on the first permission prompt OR runs unbounded tools; no max-turns causes infinite agent loops that drain budget; open stdin causes agents to read garbage from a parent pipe and behave erratically. The four-guards rule is the minimum viable contract for any headless agent invocation.

list.item: ANY cron/CI/GitHub Actions/queue/scheduled-agent invocation of claude, codex, aider, or opencode.
Multi-agent pipelines where one agent shells out to another.
Self-healing scripts that run unattended on a server.
Interactive developer sessions — the TUI and prompt-on-demand UX are the whole point; no guards required.
One-shot smoke tests where the operator is watching the terminal and will Ctrl-C on a hang.
Local prototyping where you actively want the model to stop and ask.

rule.statement: Every non-interactive invocation of claude, codex, aider, or opencode MUST set ALL four guards: (1) print/headless flag, (2) explicit tool allowlist, (3) max-turns cap, (4) < /dev/null redirect on stdin. A run missing any one of the four MUST be rejected by code review or CI lint.
rule.rationale: Each missing guard maps to a documented production failure mode (TUI hang, permission stall, runaway loop, stdin garbage). The rule is empirically the minimum that prevents mystery hangs.

list.item: OpenAI Codex: codex exec (implies headless), --sandbox workspace-write (or stricter), no native turn cap — wrap in timeout, < /dev/null.
Aider: --yes (auto-confirm), --auto-test off unless test allowlist set, --max-chat-history-tokens to bound context, < /dev/null.
opencode: headless mode flag, explicit tool config, run-time cap, < /dev/null.

antipattern.description: Using --dangerously-skip-permissions to "make it just work" in CI.
antipattern.reason: Removes guard #2 (allowlist). The agent can run arbitrary tools — including destructive bash, git push, network calls. Use a narrow allowlist instead, even if you have to enumerate it explicitly.
Defeats guard #3. The cap exists to bound cost; pick a value tied to a real workflow length (e.g. 15-30 for a single coding task) and fail loudly if hit.

rule.statement: Headless runs MUST disable the TUI explicitly (-p for Claude Code, codex exec for Codex, headless flag for others). Never rely on the binary auto-detecting non-TTY environments.
rule.rationale: Auto-detection is unreliable across container runtimes and CI shells; an unwanted TUI emits terminal control sequences that block on stdout.

example.title: No headless flag in CI
example.note: In a CI runner, the binary may attempt to start the TUI and the job hangs until job-level timeout.

rule.statement: Production headless runs MUST pass --allowedTools with the smallest viable allowlist; permission prompts in headless mode block forever.
rule.rationale: Without an allowlist the agent stalls on the first permission prompt (no human to answer) or — if --dangerously-skip-permissions was used — it executes arbitrary shell with no upper bound.

rule.statement: Every headless run MUST cap turns to a value tied to the task length; the absence of a cap is a budget bug, not a feature.
rule.rationale: Loop bugs (model decides to "try one more thing" indefinitely) drain Anthropic/OpenAI budget within minutes if the cap is open.

example.title: Open turn budget in headless run
example.note: One agent stuck in a tool-call loop can burn the daily budget cap before any human notices.

rule.statement: Always redirect < /dev/null on stdin for headless runs.
rule.rationale: Without it, the agent's stdin inherits whatever the parent shell forwards (closed pipe, garbage from another process, or interactive TTY) and behaviour becomes non-deterministic — the documented mode is closed stdin.

example.title: All four guards in place
example.note: Closed stdin guarantees the agent never blocks on a phantom read.

antipattern.description: Running the headless command inside a function that pipes JSON into stdin "just in case the agent reads it."
antipattern.reason: The CLI does not consume stdin in -p mode; piping unexpected content can change the harness's behaviour and is not part of the supported contract.

template.purpose: Reusable guarded invocation wrapper for Claude Code, Codex, Aider, opencode — sets all four guards automatically.
